text.title: SOC 2 Control Map Vendor Side Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules every vendor-side SOC 2 control map must satisfy so audit prep is a verifiable gap analysis instead of a panic project.

rule.statement: The map MUST cover all five Trust Services Criteria (Security, Availability, Processing Integrity, Confidentiality, Privacy) even if some are out of scope; out-of-scope criteria are explicitly marked, never silently omitted.
rule.rationale: Faion has SAST/DAST/supply-chain knowledge in pieces but the control-map artefact lining them up against TSC is absent. Auditors expect to see every TSC addressed (in-scope or formally out-of-scope) — a missing section forces a re-submission and stretches a 4-week prep into 6.

example.code: | TSC | In scope? | Notes | |----------------------|-----------|-------| | Security | Yes | Core controls below | | Availability | Yes | Customer SLA depends on it | | Processing Integrity | Yes | Required by enterprise buyer | | Confidentiality | Yes | Customer data is sensitive | | Privacy | No | No PII processed; auditor letter on file |

rule.statement: Every claimed control MUST cite (a) the implementing methodology / system AND (b) the exact evidence artefact the auditor will request (log export, screenshot, ticket, config file).
rule.rationale: Vendors fail audits not because controls are missing but because evidence cannot be produced in the audit window. Coupling each control to a named evidence artefact converts the map into an evidence-collection plan.

example.code: Control CC7.1 (Detection of unauthorized changes) - Implementation: SAST + DAST + signed commits (see pro/infra/cve-exception-template) - Evidence: weekly SAST report exports from GitHub Advanced Security, retained 12 months in evidence-vault/soc2-2026/CC7.1/

rule.statement: Every control row MUST name a single accountable owner (handle) responsible for maintaining the control AND producing the evidence; team-only ownership is rejected.
rule.rationale: SOC 2 auditors interview named control owners; mapping a control to "the engineering team" guarantees nobody knows it at audit time. A single named human ensures the control is actually living, not just documented.

rule.statement: Gaps (TSC requirements without an implementing control) MUST be listed explicitly with a target close-date and remediation owner; controls cannot be silently marked "compensating" without naming the compensating control.
rule.rationale: Hidden gaps are the most common audit-fail pattern. Forcing an explicit gap list with owner + date converts pre-audit denial into a managed remediation backlog and gives leadership a credible go/no-go signal for the audit window.

rule.statement: The map MUST carry a last_reviewed date and be re-walked at least every 90 days; control maps older than 90 days are treated as stale and cannot be presented to an auditor without refresh.
rule.rationale: SOC 2 Type 2 evaluates a window (typically 6 or 12 months); a stale control map at audit time means the auditor will discover drift independently and the engagement extends. Quarterly refresh keeps the map and the evidence collection synchronised.
